CHAPTER 2:
CONTEXT: WHAT CLAUDE NEEDS AND WHAT IT DOES NOT

Chapter 2: Context: What Claude Needs an — Problem Two. A manager asks Claude to write a one-paragraph project update for a status report. He pastes three previous

beats.text: Problem One. A researcher asks Claude to help draft a methods section for a paper. She provides a paragraph of background on her field, a sentence about her research question, and a long description of the statistical software she used. Claude produces a methods section that is technically well-structured but describes a study design the researcher did not use. The background she provided was too abstract , it told Claude about the field but not about the specific study. Problem Two.

title: WHAT THIS
CHAPTER BUILDS.
segments.body_html:
·Distinguish between context Claude needs, context that is neutral, and context that actively hurts.
·Organize context so Claude can prioritize it correctly.
Apply context hygienelabel sources, state assumptions, declare exclusions, remove irrelevant material.
·Identify when context is creating privacy risk and what to do about it.

beats.text: Distinguish between context Claude needs, context that is neutral, and context that actively hurts. Organize context so Claude can prioritize it correctly. Apply context hygiene: label sources, state assumptions, declare exclusions, remove irrelevant material. Identify when context is creating privacy risk and what to do about it.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER BUILDS

title: WHAT CONTEXT
ACTUALLY DOES.
segments.body_html:
·Who is the audience for this output?
·What do they already know?
·What is the source material I am drawing on?
·What assumptions should Claude make about my situation?
·What should Claude treat as background and not include in the output?

beats.text: Context in a prompt is information Claude uses to calibrate its output. It is not decoration and it is not the task. Good context answers questions the task leaves open: Who is the audience for this output? What do they already know? What is the source material I am drawing on? What assumptions should Claude make about my situation? What should Claude treat as background and not include in the output?

segments.label: 03 / 12 · WHAT CONTEXT ACTUALLY DOES

title: THE FOUR TYPES
OF CONTEXT.
segments.body_html:
·The audience for the output (a committee of finance non-specialists)
·The source material Claude should draw on (use only the attached report)
·A constraint on assumptions (assume the reader has not seen the underlying data)
·The purpose the output will serve (this will go directly into a grant proposal)
·A definition that applies in your domain but not in Claude's defaults (in our organization, "baseline" means the pre-int

beats.text: It helps to think about context in four categories. Essential context is information that would change the output materially if Claude did not have it. Without it, Claude will produce something that looks right but is wrong for the situation.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · THE FOUR TYPES OF CONTEXT

title: CONTEXT HYGIENE:
FOUR PRACTICES
segments.body_html:
When you include multiple documents or excerpts, label each one before it appears. Tell Claude what each source is, how

Context Hygiene: Four Practices —

beats.text: When you include multiple documents or excerpts, label each one before it appears. Tell Claude what each source is, how authoritative it is, and how to treat it relative to the others.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONTEXT HYGIENE: FOUR PRACTICE

title: BEFORE/AFTER:
THE PROJECT UPDATE
segments.body_html: Weak prompt:→What Claude produces:→The problem:→Specified prompt (with context hygiene applied):→Task:

beats.text: Return to the manager's scenario from the opening. Write a paragraph summarizing where we are.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · BEFORE/AFTER: THE PROJECT UPDA

title: THE PRIVACY
QUESTION.
segments.body_html:
·Does this information need to be here for the task to work?
·If so, can I anonymize or abstract it without losing the task-relevant content?
·Does my organization's data policy permit this use?

beats.text: A prompt is not a private conversation. Depending on your platform, organization settings, and usage context, prompts may be logged, reviewed, or used in model improvement processes. This matters for context hygiene in a specific way. Before including real personal data, proprietary documents, client information, or confidential details in a prompt, ask:

segments.label: 07 / 12 · THE PRIVACY QUESTION

title: CONTEXT AND
CONFLICTING SOURCES
segments.body_html:
When your context includes documents that contradict each other — earlier and later versions of a report, an email that

Context and Conflicting Sources — The professional response is to resolve the conflict explicitly before you prompt:

beats.text: When your context includes documents that contradict each other , earlier and later versions of a report, an email that corrects a document, a policy that supersedes an older one , Claude will resolve the conflict, but it will do so without telling you it did . The professional response is to resolve the conflict explicitly before you prompt:

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONTEXT AND CONFLICTING SOURCE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Pasting entire documents when you need sections.→Mixing sources without labels.→Confusing background with context.→Omitting the audience.→Including private data unnecessarily.

beats.text: Pasting entire documents when you need sections. Cut to what is relevant. Your attention to the task and Claude's attention to the task both improve when the context is specific. Mixing sources without labels. Multiple documents pasted together without labels are treated by Claude as a single undifferentiated block. Label each source before it appears.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · COMMON MISTAKES
